one_line_takeaway: TPA is the first certified (not just empirical) defense against targeted data-poisoning attacks on autoregressive language generation, formalizing stability vs. validity and using shard-and-aggregate ensembles plus a novel multi-turn MILP certificate to guarantee, e.g., that adversaries need to corrupt at least 0.5% of an agent tool-calling dataset to break validity, at the cost of substantial (150s/query) uncertified inference latency.
key_finding: Agent tool-calling (Toucan/MCP, S=500): Full LoRA achieves a median certified validity radius r=249 (0.5% of the 50k training set, equal to the theoretical maximum S/2=250) at 67% task accuracy vs. a 40% zero-shot baseline; Last-3 LoRA yields a lower median radius r=163 (0.33% resistance) at 54% accuracy but a 6x inference latency speedup, and inference latency scales from 0.3s (zero-shot) to 150s for the full ensemble on a single L40 GPU before parallelization. Preference alignment stability (Table 1, HH-RLHF, K=20 shards): DPA+ROE dominates, maintaining 42-54% First-Token Stability (FTS) at k=9 across the three models and a Stability Horizon (SH) above 3 tokens until k>5; plain DPA is weakest, e.g. Gemma2-2B falls below 10% FTS and under 1-token SH at k=9. Validity (Table 2): baseline TPA achieves a certified Validity Horizon (VH) of 20-26 tokens at k=1 but degrades to 8 tokens at k=9, whereas TPA+MSC (the paper's multi-turn/collective extension) remains far more resilient, sustaining a VH of at least 21 tokens even at k=9 and First-Token Validity (FTV) above 90% across all tested budgets k in {1,3,5,7,9}, versus standalone TPA's FTV dropping below 90% for k>=7. Empirical (uncertified) attack robustness (Table 3, PoisonBench attack, 10% global poisoning): ensembling (E, K=20) reduces Attack Success rate relative to single models (S) in every configuration tested, e.g. OLMo-1B/Tesla at T=0.8 drops from 48.9% (S) to 5.73% (E), and Qwen1.5-4B/Immigration drops from 40.5% (S) to 2.9% (E) -- this is the source of the abstract's cited "40-48% to below 6%" empirical attack-success reduction claim.
core_contribution: The paper (Ghitu & Wicker, Imperial College London) introduces the first certified-robustness framework for training-time data-poisoning defenses tailored to autoregressive natural language generation (NLG), rather than classification. It formalizes two distinct security properties: stability (no token in the generation changes under a poisoning adversary) and validity (a specific harmful token/phrase cannot be induced). Its central algorithmic contribution is Targeted Partition Aggregation (TPA), the first algorithm to certify validity by computing a sound lower bound (via Algorithm 1, proven in Theorem 4.3) on the minimum poisoning budget needed to make an adversary's target token become the ensemble's plurality vote. TPA is built on the shard-and-aggregate (ensemble/DPA) paradigm of Levine & Feizi (2020) and extended in four granularities: token-level (§4.2), sequential multi-token (§4.3), phrase-level (§4.4), and a novel multi-turn/collective certificate (§4.5, Definition 4.7, Theorem 4.8) formulated as a mixed-integer linear program (MILP) that exploits budget dilution across conversational turns -- termed TPA+MSC (multi-sample certification) for validity and DPA+MSC for stability.
threat_model: White-box, unbounded poisoning adversary who can arbitrarily modify features, labels, or both in up to r training points (bounded via symmetric-difference ball B_r(D)), with full knowledge of architecture, dataset, training order, and hyperparameters (worst-case certification assumption). The adversary's capability is training-time only -- the paper explicitly scopes out test-time evasion, prompt injection, and pre-existing triggered backdoors as separate, out-of-scope threat modes (stated explicitly in the Impact Statement). Two adversarial objectives are distinguished: untargeted (maximize probability of any output change -- stability) and targeted (maximize probability of inducing a specific harmful output y* -- validity). The paper argues these objectives diverge sharply in NLG (unlike classification) because the output space |V|^L is exponentially large, so preventing arbitrary changes does not imply preventing specific harmful ones.
limitations: The authors explicitly state inference-time latency remains an open challenge: the full-ensemble sequential certification requires 150s per response on a single GPU versus 0.3s for zero-shot inference (only mitigated by hardware parallelization or by partial-model fine-tuning such as Last-3 LoRA, which weakens the guarantees). Certificates are worst-case and thus conservative -- the empirical attack-success results (Table 3) are presented separately precisely because certified bounds are much more pessimistic than what practical adversaries achieve. The Impact Statement explicitly scopes the threat model to training-time poisoning only, excluding test-time evasion, prompt-injection attacks, and triggered/backdoor attacks as "other important threat modes" left as open challenges. The validity experiments on HH-RLHF use an intentionally conservative evaluation setup (assuming the model has already generated the first m tokens of the unsafe string) rather than testing unconditional generation of the full harmful sequence. Phrase-level certification is noted to become looser (vote dilution across |V|^L classes) as phrase length approaches the full sequence length L.

datasets_used: Toucan (Xu et al., 2025) filtered to top 150 tools, 50,000 training instances, for agent tool-calling/MCP experiments
Anthropic HH-RLHF (Bai et al., 2022; Ganguli et al., 2022) for preference-based alignment (DPO) stability/validity experiments
quantitative_results: Agent tool-calling: Full LoRA median certified validity radius r=249 (0.5% of 50k dataset), 67% accuracy; Last-3 LoRA median r=163 (0.33%), 54% accuracy; zero-shot baseline 40% accuracy
Latency: 0.3s zero-shot vs 150s for S=500 ensemble on single L40 GPU; Last-3 LoRA gives up to 5.9x speedup over Full LoRA
HH-RLHF stability (Table 1, K=20, k=9): DPA+ROE FTS 42-54% across OLMo-1B/Gemma2-2B/Qwen1.5-4B; plain DPA FTS as low as 9.6% (Gemma2-2B); DPA+ROE SH stays above 3 tokens until k>5

HH-RLHF validity (Table 2, K=20): baseline TPA VH 20-26 tokens at k=1, dropping to 8 tokens at k=9; TPA+MSC VH remains >=21 tokens at k=9; TPA+MSC FTV >90% for all k in {1,3,5,7,9}; standalone TPA FTV <90% for k>=7

Empirical attack robustness (Table 3, PoisonBench, 10% poisoning): Attack Success rate reduced from 40-48% (single model) to below 6% (K=20 ensemble) across entities/temperatures; specific example OLMo-1B/Tesla/T=0.8: 48.9% (single) to 5.73% (ensemble); Qwen1.5-4B/Immigration/T=0.8: 40.5% to 2.9%
baselines_compared: Deep Partition Aggregation (DPA) (Levine & Feizi, 2020) - classification-oriented shard-and-aggregate certification, used as stability baseline
DPA+ROE (run-off elections, Rezaei et al., 2023) - stronger stability baseline
Zero-shot (no ensemble) inference - accuracy/latency baseline in tool-calling experiments
Single (non-ensembled) model - empirical attack-success baseline in Table 3
The paper's own DPA+MSC, TPA, and TPA+MSC are compared against these external baselines, not against each other as external baselines
